hook: A vertiginous medieval stepwell hidden among the corporate towers off Connaught Place
facts: 60m long, 15m wide, 108 stone steps descending in three tiers
Attributed to the legendary king Agrasen and rebuilt in the 14th century
An ASI-protected monument now dry, surrounded by glass office blocks
Featured in the film PK, which drew crowds to the once-quiet ruin
legend: Locals say the black water that once filled it lured the depressed to drown themselves
They say the walls echo footsteps and a presence follows visitors down the steps
w: Drop off a Connaught Place pavement and 108 stone steps fall away into cool dark between glass office towers. A baoli rebuilt in the 14th century, dry now, its arched galleries swallowing the city noise. Legend says the black water it once held drew the despairing to drown. Stairs that go down too far.

hook: Tughlaq ruins where petitioners leave letters for djinns every Thursday
facts: Citadel of Firozabad, built circa 1354 by Feroz Shah Tughlaq
Crowned by a polished 3rd-century BCE Ashokan pillar moved from Topra
Every Thursday people leave letters, incense and food in its cellars
ASI-protected; the djinn cult grew largely from the 1970s onward
legend: Believers say djinns hold a midnight court here to judge the written petitions
The chief spirit, 'lath wale baba', is said to dwell by the Ashokan pillar
w: Tughlaq's crumbling citadel, an Ashokan pillar rising polished from the rubble. Come Thursday the cellars fill with incense, food and letters, photocopied many times over, addressed to djinns. Believers say the spirits hold court at midnight to weigh each petition. A post office for the unseen.

hook: A jewel-box Lodi mosque whose painted tomb chamber locals tie to djinns
facts: Built around 1528-1529; the poet-saint Jamali buried here after 1535
Tomb ceiling covered in painted and tiled plasterwork, rare survival
Kamali's identity remains unknown, buried beside Jamali
Set deep in the Mehrauli Archaeological Park, often locked
legend: They say the name comes from two djinns, Jamaluddin and Kamaluddin, still in residence
Visitors report growls, laughing voices and a presence among the pillars
w: A small red mosque from the 1520s, and beside it a tomb chamber painted ceiling to floor in faded blue and gold, holding the Sufi poet Jamali and a man named Kamali no one can identify. They say two djinns of the same names still live in the jharokhas. People hear laughing in the empty room.

hook: A serene four-tier Lodi stepwell that masons once lived in
facts: Commissioned 1506 by Daulat Khan, an administrator under the Lodis
Name 'rajon' refers to the masons (raj) who reportedly used it
Three storeys of arched colonnades descend to green water
A small mosque and chhatri tomb stand on the upper terrace
w: Tucked in the Mehrauli scrub, a stepwell from 1506 with three storeys of arched arcades stepping down to still green water. Its name nods to the masons said to have lodged in its galleries. A domed tomb and tiny mosque keep watch above. Quiet enough to hear the parakeets crossing it.

hook: A roofless ruin holding the first true arch in India
facts: Tomb of Ghiyas ud din Balban, Sultan of Delhi, died 1287
Credited with the earliest true arch and dome in Indo-Islamic India
Now a stark roofless shell of rubble masonry
Stands within the Mehrauli Archaeological Park
w: A roofless grey shell among the Mehrauli ruins, easy to walk past. Yet its empty doorway frames the first true arch ever raised in India, around 1287, the keystone trick that would later float every Mughal dome. The Sultan Balban lies under open sky. Architecture's quiet starting gun.

hook: A maze-walled tomb for a general Akbar had thrown off a roof
facts: Tomb of Adham Khan, executed in 1562 on Akbar's orders
Akbar had him flung twice from the Agra Fort ramparts
Honeycombed inner passages give it the name Bhool Bhulaiyaa, the labyrinth
Once used by the British as a residence and a police lockup
legend: Locals say the spirits of Adham Khan and his mother Maham Anga still wander the maze
w: Akbar had this general thrown off the Agra ramparts twice for murder, then built him a domed tomb on a Mehrauli knoll. Its walls riddled with passages that lose you, hence Bhool Bhulaiyaa, the maze. The British later lived in it, then jailed people there. They say two ghosts still circle the corridors.

hook: A Sufi saint's shrine where qawwali still rises every Thursday night
facts: Shrine of Nizamuddin Auliya, the Chishti Sufi saint who died 1325
Qawwali singing here traces a line back to his disciple Amir Khusrau
The poet Khusrau and princess Jahanara lie buried in the same enclosure
Reached through a warren of incense and rose-petal lanes off the main road
legend: They say the saint cursed a sultan's tower; 'Delhi is far away', and the sultan died en route
w: Thread the rose-petal lanes and the marble shrine of a saint dead seven centuries opens out, and on Thursday nights qawwali swells until the crowd sways. Amir Khusrau, who shaped the music, lies a few feet away. Legend says the saint cursed a sultan: 'Delhi is far away.' The sultan never reached it.

hook: A 128-room merchant mansion still lived in by one family on Chandni Chowk
facts: Built 1848 by Lala Chunnamal, a brocade and shawl magnate
Covers an acre, 128 rooms across three storeys behind a long balcony
He supplied the British in 1857 and became Delhi's richest man
Descendants still occupy part of it, rarely open to the public
w: Above the chaos of Chandni Chowk a long iron-railed balcony hides 128 rooms and an acre of mansion, raised in 1848 by a shawl merchant who read 1857 correctly and ended Delhi's richest man. Belgian chandeliers, painted ceilings, his descendants still inside. Faded grandeur with the lights still on.

hook: Asia's largest wholesale spice bazaar, named for a vanished stepwell
facts: Operating on this street since around the 17th century
Reputed to be Asia's largest wholesale spice market
Name means 'salty stepwell', after a baoli long gone
Air thick with chilli dust that sets newcomers coughing
w: Sacks of red chilli, cardamom and dried rose stacked head-high down a lane working since the 1600s, reputedly Asia's biggest wholesale spice market. The chilli dust hangs so thick that first-timers stand in the street and cough. Named for a salty stepwell no one can find anymore. Breathe shallow.

hook: The poet Mirza Ghalib's last home, rescued from being a coal store
facts: Home of the Urdu and Persian poet Mirza Ghalib, died 1869
Tucked in Gali Qasim Jan off Ballimaran in the walled city
Once degraded into shops and a coal depot before restoration
Now a small ASI memorial museum, closed Mondays, free entry
w: Down Gali Qasim Jan, a fragment of the house where Mirza Ghalib spent his last years, writing couplets that Delhi still quotes in arguments and weddings. For decades it sank into shops and a coal depot before being clawed back. A life-size Ghalib now sits with his hookah. The poet, gone, still at home.

hook: A Tughlaq hunting lodge swallowed by ridge forest, fenced and feared after dark
facts: A 14th-century hunting lodge linked to Feroz Shah Tughlaq
Buried in the Central Ridge forest off Karol Bagh
One of three ridge lodges, with Pir Ghaib and Malcha Mahal
Reached by a lonely track and closed to visitors after dusk
legend: They say a woman's spirit haunts it and scratches those who linger past sunset
w: A 14th-century hunting lodge drowned in ridge forest minutes from Karol Bagh, reached by a dirt track the city forgets. The name slurs from a Sufi's or a lost herdswoman's. Either way locals clear out by dusk, when they say a presence circles the courtyard and scratches whoever stays. Don't be the one who stays.

hook: A lonely Ridge tower where British families huddled on the night of the 1857 revolt
facts: Round signal tower built around 1828 on the Northern Ridge
British residents sheltered here as Delhi rose on 11 May 1857
Set in the wooded Ridge reserve forest of north Delhi
An ASI-protected relic, usually shuttered and silent
w: A squat round tower in the Ridge woods, easy to miss among the peacocks. On 11 May 1857 the panicked British of Delhi crammed inside it as the revolt broke, before fleeing north. It saw the empire wobble in a single afternoon, then went quiet for good. History happened here, then everyone left.

hook: A Gothic spire to the 1857 dead, re-dedicated by free India to the other side
facts: Red Gothic tower raised in 1863 for British and Indian troops killed in 1857
Stands on the Northern Ridge above the old siege lines
In 1972 India renamed it Ajitgarh and added a counter-plaque
The new plaque honours those the British called rebels as freedom fighters
w: A red Gothic spire on the Ridge, built in 1863 to mourn the soldiers who died retaking Delhi in 1857. In 1972 free India left the tower standing but bolted on a new plaque, recasting the 'rebels' as freedom fighters and renaming it Ajitgarh. One monument arguing with itself in stone.

hook: The 'Bloody Gate' marooned on a traffic island, drenched in execution lore
facts: A Sher Shah Suri-era gateway, mid-16th century
Mughal princes were shot here by a British officer in 1857
Some accounts tie earlier killings and displayed heads to the gate
Now stranded on a median of Bahadur Shah Zafar Marg
legend: They say on damp nights blood is seen seeping from its old stones
w: A lone red gate stranded on a traffic median, the cars indifferent. The Mughal sons of Bahadur Shah Zafar were stripped and shot here in 1857, and older blood is claimed for it too, hence Khooni Darwaza, the Bloody Gate. They say the stones still weep red on wet nights. Mind the traffic to reach it.

hook: A cyclopean ghost-fort cursed, the story goes, to lie empty forever
facts: Built 1321-1325 by Ghiyas ud din Tughlaq, founder of the dynasty
Sloping rubble ramparts run for some 6km in a vast circuit
Abandoned within years of completion, never truly reoccupied
Now a quiet sprawl of bastions roamed by monkeys and goatherds
legend: They say the saint Nizamuddin cursed it: 'May it stay desolate, or be peopled only by herdsmen'
w: Six kilometres of sloping stone rampart thrown up in the 1320s, then emptied almost at once. Legend pins it on the saint Nizamuddin, who, feuding with the sultan, supposedly cursed it to stand desolate or hold only herdsmen. Today: monkeys, goats and wind through the bastions. The curse looks like it took.

hook: A 14th-century royal reservoir and seminary fronting a lake full of birds
facts: Reservoir first dug under Alauddin Khalji around 1300 for Siri city
Feroz Shah Tughlaq added a madrasa and his own tomb by 1354
The L-shaped seminary steps right down to the water's edge
The deer park and lake draw herons, owls and weekend painters
w: Behind the boutiques and bars, a 14th-century reservoir still holds water, and a long arcaded seminary steps straight down into it, the sultan Feroz Shah's tomb anchoring one corner. Cormorants dry their wings on the ruins while students sketch. A royal water tank that outlived three vanished cities.

hook: A Mughal pleasure garden reborn around forgotten tombs
facts: Laid out as a Mughal garden, Azim Bagh, in the 16th century
Holds several Mughal-era tombs, six of them ASI-protected
Restored and reopened in 2018 over some 90 acres
Sits on the old Grand Trunk Road beside Humayun's Tomb
w: A 16th-century Mughal garden gone to seed for centuries, then coaxed back to ninety acres of water channels and lawns, with half a dozen tumbledown tombs left standing among the roses. Butterflies, a heritage rose bed, and a domed grave you can picnic beside. The Mughals built it to stroll in; you still can.

hook: A walled colonial graveyard holding the brigadier who stormed Delhi in 1857
facts: Christian cemetery in use from the early 19th century
Named for Brigadier John Nicholson, mortally wounded in 1857
Hundreds of graves of soldiers, missionaries and infants
A green hush behind walls beside the Kashmere Gate bus terminus
w: Behind a wall by the roaring Kashmere Gate bus stand lies a green colonial graveyard, headstones tilting under old trees. Its star resident: Brigadier John Nicholson, the zealot who breached Delhi in 1857 and died of his wound days later. Around him, rows of fever-killed infants and forgotten subalterns.

hook: A surviving city gate named for a saint, scarred by a 1976 demolition
facts: One of the few surviving gates of Shahjahanabad, mid-17th century
Named for the Sufi saint Hazrat Shah Turkman Bayabani nearby
Site of deadly police firing during the 1976 Emergency clearances
Now a lone stone arch islanded in Old Delhi traffic
w: A 17th-century gate of the walled city, named for a Sufi whose grave sits just behind it. In 1976, during the Emergency, bulldozers came to clear the slums around it and police fired on protesters; the death toll is still argued over. The arch stands on, carrying two histories: Mughal stone, modern wound.

hook: A 'cloud mosque' built by Aurangzeb's daughter, near where she lies
facts: Built around 1707 by Zinat-un-Nissa, daughter of Aurangzeb
Three striped marble domes earn it the name Ghata Masjid, cloud mosque
British troops used it as a bakery after 1857
Tucked in the back lanes of Daryaganj near the old riverbank
w: Down a Daryaganj lane, three marble domes striped in red sandstone seem to drift, hence Ghata Masjid, the cloud mosque, raised around 1707 by Aurangzeb's daughter Zinat-un-Nissa. After 1857 British soldiers turned it into a bakery. The princess once lay buried in its court. A daughter's mosque, twice betrayed.

hook: A Tughlaq lodge in deep ridge jungle, home to a tragic self-styled royal recluse
facts: A 14th-century Tughlaq hunting lodge in the Central Ridge forest
From 1985 occupied by Begum Wilayat, claimant of the Oudh royal house
She died in 1993; her son Ali Raza lived on alone until 2017
Reached only by a long forest track off Sardar Patel Marg
legend: Locals say the begum's spirit and her dogs still guard the ruined corridors
w: A 14th-century hunting lodge lost in ridge jungle off Chanakyapuri's embassy roads. For thirty years a woman calling herself the Begum of Oudh lived here in candlelit exile with her two children and a pack of dogs, pressing a doomed royal claim. She died in 1993; her son haunted it until 2017. Then no one.

hook: A faith with no idols, no clergy and no sermons, housed in 27 marble petals
facts: Bahai House of Worship completed in 1986 on the village land of Bahapur
Twenty-seven marble-clad 'petals' in nine groups of three
Open to all faiths; no images, sermons or fixed rituals inside
Among the most-visited buildings on earth, yet kept in silence
w: Everyone photographs the petals; almost no one knows the creed inside. The Bahai faith keeps no idols, no priests, no sermons, so the marble hall holds only silence and whoever cares to sit. Built in 1986 on a village called Bahapur, it draws millions a year to do, deliberately, nothing at all. Quiet by design.
